speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Voorzanger, would you continue? You were talking about your discharge from the Armed Services.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. When I had no clothing, because I was out grown everything in two years. And so I had to buy clothing to go back to civilian life. So I had to use the money for my father's life insurance, worth the half of it. And my brother was very mad at me, and he didn't talk to me for years. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I didn't talk to my brother, or hear from him through 1954 [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] at the funeral of Mr. [NON-ENGLISH]. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] And he-- he refused to shake hands with me at the funeral. He didn't talk to me then.

speaker: interviewer
text: So let's go back, though, to that time just after you were discharged. Did you have any place you could go to?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went back to live with my girlfriend. And I got my same job, which I had before I went into the Air Force. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then I switched jobs to get a better salary within the same line. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I did that for maybe a year or-- or so, I don't recall exactly. And then I went to work for a department store. Very famous department store in the Hague. The Bijenkorf-- in the photo department. And I became a salesman there in the photo department. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] then in 1954, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] my girlfriend told me that she was pregnant. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So we made plans to get married. And we got married in the Hague October. It was October 29th, I believe. And-- Could you tell me her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name was Ellie, short for Elizabeth. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And she worked for the KLM. And I had my job at the department store. And we got married. But in February of 1955, she became ill. And the doctors didn't know what was wrong with her. So they decided to hospitalize her. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And on the way to the hospital, I was following the ambulance with my bicycle on a snowy morning, Saturday morning, February 19th.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I came into the hospital, and the nurse came to me and she said Mr. Voorzanger? And I said yes. She said you're the father of a healthy boy. I said, what? And she said, yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: She was only about seven months pregnant, though. So they put my son in the incubator. My wife was very ill. And gradually, she became better. And on Monday night I went to see her in the hospital, and she was talking, sitting up in bed. She decided to name my son Fritz, after me. I argued with her a little bit about that. But she didn't want to listen to me. So I said Fritz it is. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And the next morning I went to City Hall, and I announced his birth at City Hall. And I had birth-- birth announcements printed up. And Tuesday night, I went to visit her, I went to her room. And I said Ellie-- I called her Elsie. I said, Elsie, it's me. And she looked at me and she passed away. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I wouldn't know why, what happened. The doctor wanted an autopsy. I refused. And we never found out why. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So I sent a telegram to my brother. I had his address. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He never came to the funeral. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: In the meantime, I had to find somebody to take care of my son. So I had some very good friends, one of whom was a man I used to work with in the darkroom. And they had no children of their own. And the woman said, you know, if you don't have anybody from the family who wants to take care of Freddy-- we called him Freddy-- we'll take care of him. So that's what we did. She took care of him for two years.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your state of mind at that time, with having that loss so close to all the other losses that you'd had?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went out of my mind. I became very [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] rebellious. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I remember at one particular point when I was working in the department store, I wanted to buy a Rolleiflex. It was selling the camera. It was very expensive. But we got special discounts. I wanted to take pictures of my son. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I was looking at the camera every day, every day, and trying to save money to pay for it afterwards. And one day we came into the department store, the camera was gone. Somebody stole it. And I was number uno suspect.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they dragged me to the police department, and they questioned me for a half a day-- threatening me. The police questions [INAUDIBLE]. So they let me go.

speaker: interviewee
text: But of course, the whole department store knew who Jack Voorzanger was, especially since my wife had passed away. You know, they knew that. [INAUDIBLE] burning fire through the whole place. And about two weeks later, they finally found the man who had stolen the camera. He had been stealing from all departments. He was one of the janitors.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I went upstairs. I went to the director of the company. And I demanded that he write on the bulletin board the fact that I was found, you know, in error.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he didn't want to do that. So I quit. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And I went to work for an insurance company. And I became a foreign correspondent for a huge insurance company [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] until I emigrated to the United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now did you have anybody there that you could confide in, or--?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your wife's family of any help to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. My wife's sister, she had their own life. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And she had-- they had three little boys, brothers. They were too little to do anything. But she never lifted a finger. She never did anything. That's why I never contacted her or nothing. The same with my father-in-law. But when he-- I bought a little camera to take pictures of my son. And he was a milk delivery man. And he came every day. He brought me a bottle of milk for Freddy. When he found out I had bought a camera, he said, you're wasting your money on cameras. He said, you know, you can buy your own milk. And that's the kind of a family. So I never contacted him after I immigrated or anything. Nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your uncle?

speaker: interviewee
text: My uncle, [COUGHS] I lost all contact with him after the day that he kicked me out. But somehow he got to hear that Ellie had passed away, and he wrote me a letter. I should come and visit him. And maybe he can help me. So I went to visit him. I said, what the heck?

speaker: interviewee
text: So my aunt wanted me to bring the baby to her to take care of him. And I flatly refused that, of course. But I came to visit him regularly once a week. And on one evening he said, guess what? I said what. He said guess who I received a letter from? I said, I don't know. From whom? He said, Rika [NON-ENGLISH]. I said, Rika. Which Rika? He said, your cousin. Cousin? He said, cousin from whom?

speaker: interviewee
text: He said she was Uncle Isaax's daughter. So he said here's a letter. And I read a letter that her husband, Bernard, had written to my uncle. And in the letter he wrote that he lived in the United States, in Culver City. And he said that if he-- if my uncle knew of any Voorzangers still alive, to please to let him know [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] so he could arrange to him-- to have them immigrate to the United States. And he would gladly sponsor it.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he gave me that letter. It was in 1955. Yeah, 1955. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Right away I wrote him, because to go emigrate. I wanted to get out of Holland. I had it with that country. You know, nothing but misfortunes. So I wrote him a letter. And he said OK, fine. Told me what to do. And he was going to pay for my trip, and for Freddy, my son.

speaker: interviewee
text: Went to the consulate, and asked for a visa. And I tried to get a visa under the refugee program, in the United States Immigration Department. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] But it took so long that I started losing interest coming to the United States-- until 1956, when the Russians invaded Hungary. And my brother and I, we had made up. I forgot to mention that. He came to visit me in the department store to say hello. So we had made up, kind of. So my brother said to me-- I came-- I went to visit him in Belgium, [? or ?] he came to visit me in Holland. He said get the hell out of here. He said, the Russians-- he said we're going to go through the same thing we've been through in the '40s. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I lost my cousin's address. I wrote to the consulate, asking if they could give me his address. They said no. They said I could apply with a church or some charitable organization to sponsor me. With some luck, I found his address. So I wrote him again. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Unfortunately, he said I cannot pay for your trip, because we just bought a house, and I invested all my money in it. But I will still sponsor you.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I went to the consulate in Amsterdam-- in Rotterdam. I applied-- reapplied for the visa. I had a medical. They gave my son a medical. And in April '57, [SNAP] we took off from Amsterdam with KLM. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] And I never [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] had any regrets of leaving the country. Except for one thing-- food. [LAUGHS] Before we get on to, you know, your life in the United States, I just wanted to ask you about Mr. [NON-ENGLISH] funeral, and how you felt about going there. But perhaps I should ask you why you went there.

speaker: interviewee
text: My wife?

speaker: interviewer
text: Why. Why you went there.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh. Well, the man mistreated me terribly, and he ruined me mentally. Because I had all the education that I had. I had just-- after I left him. But he still, he saved my life. If it-- if it wouldn't be for him, I wouldn't be here today. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] So I thought it was my duty to go and go to his funeral. And I think my brother had the same feeling. That's why I met him at the funeral. Unfortunately, it was not a very good meeting because he didn't talk to me, and he refused to shake hands with me. And I went back to Holland the same day. I didn't see him until way after my wife passed away in 1955.

speaker: interviewer
text: At the funeral, did any of the [NON-ENGLISH] family show any remorse about what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. The only thing I recall is that one of his brothers who came to the funeral, they were looking at us, and they said-- they asked a woman, who are these guys? And she told them. He said, oh yeah, I remember. Those two-- the-- the Jewish boys who were here. And that's about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you ever have any other contact with that family?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went back a couple of times after he passed away, you know. But then-- then I went to the United States and I lost all contact with them. And when I went back to Belgium in 1984 with my wife and my two daughters, we went to visit [NON-ENGLISH], and I wanted to show them the house where I was hiding during the war. They were not living there anymore.

speaker: interviewee
text: But the neighbor was still there. I recognized the neighbor. And she told me the woman was still alive. And she said she lives there and there. Would you like to go and visit her? And I said no, I don't think so. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I have not contacted any of the family any more. So let's go back now to when you arrived in the United States. Can you describe that period of time in your life?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was very happy, of course, to come here, and especially since my cousin. Then he is a relative-- the only relative besides my brother left over from-- from the Holocaust. I got a very good job. I was-- I arrived here on a Friday. And Monday morning I already had a job with a photographer. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] then I got another job in Pasadena as a manager in a photo lab. Made good salary. I was laid off in December. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] I had my car repossessed. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I was on unemployment. And then I met a very famous photographer. His name was Robert Cleveland. Architectural photographer. And he proposed to me that he wanted to open a professional photo lab, and if I would be interested in running it as a partnership. And I immediately accepted, because I had nothing else. And that was the beginning of a very good career that I started in 1957-- or 1958, February. Opened a photo lab under the name of Professional Photo Processing. And I ran that photo lab by myself. First, Bob Cleveland, he left the partnership, because he said it was too much-- too much of a drain on him financially. He had to pay too many taxes, because the business was booming. So I ran the place by myself. I took it over for 36 years. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And I just sold it about-- actually I didn't sell it. [? Not ?] much money. We went in '94, because the business went right [? down. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: But during that early time, when you had just arrived here, was it difficult making the adjustment?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very difficult.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can we talk about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: You mean adjustment?

speaker: interviewer
text: The adjustment to-- from coming from Holland.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I didn't have any problems with the adjustment. I was used to being adjusted to life in any circumstance. So this was a piece of cake for me here. I spoke the language.

speaker: interviewer
text: How had you learned English?

speaker: interviewee
text: I learned it myself by buying newspapers. I bought it in Holland. And I bought English books, newspapers. listened to the radio, English broadcast. So when-- when I came here I was prepared, because I knew I was coming. So I studied by myself to learn the language of course. You don't speak it. My vocabulary was OK, but my grammar-- you know. But I managed.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who was taking care of your child?

speaker: interviewee
text: My cousin, Rika, was taking care of my son, Freddy. And-- And everybody kept saying to me, you know, you need a mother. You need a mother. You have to get married. Your child needs a mother. And I had no-- no desire to get married. So I have some friends here who were living in Century City. And they told me, you know, we have a beautiful girl, and she's good for you. And he kept pressuring, pressuring, pressing me. So I finally I wrote to her in Brussels. And she emigrated in 1958. She came here in December. And in 1959 in February we got married.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Elizabeth. Three Elizabeths in my life. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Then I had a daughter with her-- my daughter Karen.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was she born?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was born in November-- November 30, 1959. And the marriage was not a very good one. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I think I was-- I didn't really know her, you know. So after six years we decided to get a divorce. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I continued my photo lab. I had a place on La Cienega Boulevard here. 355 North. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And I put an ad in a magazine, professional photographers magazine, it was called a range finder. And I needed some help. Because I had too much work. I was doing it all myself. I had to take care of the people in the front and do the work in the back. So I put an ad. And one day the beautiful girl walks in. And she was asking for the job. And I told her, yes, I need somebody for the front. So she misunderstood. She thought I said I need a friend for the front. [CHUCKLES] Anyhow, I hired her. That was in 1966. And we got married in March 25, 1968.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name is [INAUDIBLE] Juliette. And we're celebrating our 29th wedding anniversary coming March 25th. We have two beautiful girls, Olivia and Vanessa.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when were they born?

speaker: interviewee
text: Vanessa-- Olivia was born September 21, 1970. And Vanessa was born July 1, 1973. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And Olivia has married. And Karen is married. And Karen has a little girl, Ashley. And Olivia just had a little boy, [NON-ENGLISH]. Four months old. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] So it's a little happiness, too, in life.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now can you talk more about your experiences after you came here, and how you fared. And what happened in your family and so on?

speaker: interviewee
text: With my uncle, with my-- with my cousin?

speaker: interviewer
text: No. During the time that you lived in Los Angeles.

speaker: interviewee
text: What [INAUDIBLE] time period is that? From the day I arrived here, you mean? I lived with my cousins in 1957 I came here. And then in 1958, when I opened this photo lab with Bob Cleveland, I rented a little apartment here on Waring for ten dollars a week. And we struggled in the beginning financially to build up the business. And then gradually, the business built up. And-- and financially, I was doing very well.

speaker: interviewer
text: During that time--

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm sorry.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's OK. When my brother came, he followed me. He came in 19-- December 1957. He came here with his wife Sonia. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] right away he got a good job in a bank here. He became a cashier in the [INAUDIBLE] Citizens Bank, I believe it was at the time. And his wife had a job at a bank. And we were very close during that period. But something happened. Something snapped somewhere. Because of frictions between my-- my wife Betty and my brother Joe.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was your second wife.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. So again, we parted company for a while. And we didn't see each other [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] for almost five years. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And then when I got divorced I called him. And again, we-- we made up. And we became close again. And we visited each other. And in the meantime, I was running my business. It was good. I had a very good clientele. I had very famous photographers coming to-- to my door. I was doing very well financially. Family wise, it was not so good, because my brother was the only one I had, and we didn't talk to each other. I went through a terrible divorce. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So the struggle continued mentally.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you talk about that a little bit more?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. As I told before, I loved my brother very dearly. But there were constant frictions. The littlest thing, you know, we would jump at each other. And we just didn't get along any more. We just didn't get along. He was criticizing me, I was criticizing him. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I went through a couple of things I asked him to do for me, he flatly refused. Financial things. Asked him to if I could borrow money from him. He said no. When my son was here, and I was living by myself, and my son was living with a family somewhere else, I asked him if I could bring my son over on Sunday, because I want him close to me. And he said no. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] A lot of things, you know. A lot of friction. A little painful.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Voorzanger, we're going to end this tape here.
